video_sections.label: GNP and GDP
video_sections.content_summary: Many measures beyond just core/periphery/semi-periphery. GNP (Gross National Product): total value of goods/services a nation's CITIZENS produce regardless of location (Toyota cars made in US count toward Japan's GNP — emphasis on citizens). GDP (Gross Domestic Product): total value produced WITHIN a country's borders regardless of who produces it (Toyota cars made in US count toward US GDP — emphasis on location). GDP has become the more reliable measure of economic development.

mcq.question: What is the key difference between GDP and GNP?
options: GDP measures income while GNP measures the value of goods produced
GDP measures goods produced within a country's borders; GNP measures goods produced by a country's citizens regardless of location
GDP only applies to core countries while GNP applies to all countries
GDP includes informal economic activity while GNP does not
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. GDP focuses on location (everything produced within a country's borders), while GNP focuses on citizens (everything produced by a country's citizens, regardless of where they produce it).
mcq.feedback_incorrect: GDP measures the total value of goods and services produced WITHIN a country's borders. GNP measures the total value produced by a country's CITIZENS regardless of where in the world that production occurs.

video_sections.label: GNI and Income Measures
video_sections.content_summary: Note: ad read ~2:27-2:47. GNI (Gross National Income): total income earned by a country's businesses and labor, including GDP plus overseas earnings and foreign direct investment — better for assessing developing countries because they receive FDI from core countries. GNI per capita: GNI divided by population (average individual income); doesn't account for actual income distribution gap. Income distribution: smaller gap between rich and poor = more developed (Brazil: 1% holds 50% of wealth = massive gap).

mcq.question: Why is GNI considered a better measure than GDP for assessing developing countries?
options: GNI only counts the formal economic sector, giving more accurate data for developing countries
GNI accounts for foreign direct investment that developing countries receive from core countries
GNI divides total income by population, showing average individual wealth levels
GNI measures the value of goods produced by a country's citizens regardless of location
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. Developing countries often receive significant foreign direct investment from core countries. GNI captures this investment alongside GDP and overseas earnings, giving a more complete picture of economic standing than GDP alone.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: GNI is better for assessing developing countries because it accounts for foreign direct investment from core countries. While GDP only captures production within borders — which may look bleak for developing nations — GNI includes this outside investment for a more accurate assessment.

video_sections.label: Demographic and Sector Indicators
video_sections.content_summary: Fertility rate (lower = more developed, women have more education access). Infant mortality rate (lower = more developed, more healthcare access). Access to healthcare (more = more developed, longer lifespans). As GDP/GNP/GNI increase, country becomes healthier and wealthier. Economic sector structure (tertiary+ = more developed). Formal vs informal sectors: formal = registered businesses (Walmart); informal = economic activity outside government oversight (undocumented workers, cash babysitting). Informal sectors undermine accuracy of GDP/GNI.

mcq.question: Why is a large informal economic sector an indicator of less development?
options: Because informal sectors only exist in countries with no international trade agreements
Because informal economic activity operates outside government oversight, undermining accurate measurement and indicating less economic structure
Because informal sectors only involve agricultural activities in the primary sector
Because countries with large informal sectors have higher GDP per capita than core countries
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. Informal sectors operate outside government oversight (undocumented work, cash transactions). They undermine the accuracy of measures like GDP and indicate less economic structure — in some peripheral countries, informal activity constitutes almost half the economy.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: A large informal sector indicates less development because that economic activity operates outside government oversight and regulation. In peripheral countries, informal sectors can represent almost half the economy — measures like GDP only capture the formal sector.

video_sections.label: GII and HDI
video_sections.content_summary: Informal sector prevalence: core countries ~10-20% additional value, peripheral countries can be almost half the economy (street vendor kiosk example). Larger informal = less developed. Gender Inequality Index (GII): measures equality/inequality between men and women — three components: reproductive health (maternal mortality, adolescent birth rates), empowerment (women in parliament, higher education), labor market participation. Higher GII = less developed (inverse relationship). Human Development Index (HDI): created by United Nations, combines GDP + life expectancy + education + literacy rates — more holistic measure of human well-being than purely economic measures.

mcq.question: What does the Human Development Index (HDI) measure?
options: Only the total value of goods and services produced within a country's borders
The ratio of men to women in a country's workforce
A holistic measure combining GDP, life expectancy, education, and literacy rates
The percentage of a country's economy devoted to commodity exports
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. The HDI, created by the United Nations, is a holistic measure that goes beyond economics to include GDP, life expectancy, education levels, and literacy rates — assessing overall human well-being.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: The Human Development Index combines GDP with life expectancy, education, and literacy rates to give a more holistic picture of development. It was created by the United Nations to capture dimensions of well-being that purely economic measures miss.

video_sections.label: Girls' Education and Development
video_sections.content_summary: General rule: as countries develop economically, women's roles change. Strong connection between development and girls' education — more developed = more access, stay in school longer. Historically male children preferred (better job/income prospects), educating girls seen as a waste. This rigid gender hierarchy present in least developed countries. In the most developed economies, women enroll in college at higher rates than men (US: ~60/40). Note: ad read ~1:11-1:15.

mcq.question: In the most developed economies, what pattern has emerged in college enrollment by gender?
options: Men enroll at significantly higher rates due to better job prospects
Women enroll at higher rates than men, with about a 60/40 ratio in the United States
Enrollment is perfectly equal due to government-mandated gender quotas
College enrollment has declined for both genders as vocational training increased
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. In the most developed economies, women actually enroll in college at higher rates than men. In the United States, the ratio is about 60% women to 40% men averaged across all states.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: In the most developed economies, women now enroll in college at higher rates than men. In the United States, the percentage of women to men is about 60/40 — a reversal of historical patterns where male children were preferred for education.

video_sections.label: Demographic Effects on Women
video_sections.content_summary: Increasing levels of development have significant demographic effects on women. As development increases and more women participate in higher education, they delay marriage and have fewer children → declining fertility rates and slower population growth. Population pyramids for developed countries show declining populations; less developed countries show women having far more children (Nigeria: 6+ children average).

mcq.question: What happens to fertility rates as countries become more economically developed?
options: Fertility rates increase because families can afford more children
Fertility rates stay the same regardless of development level
Fertility rates tend to decline because women have more access to education and delay marriage
Fertility rates increase temporarily then decline after population doubles
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. As countries develop, women gain more access to education and career opportunities, leading them to delay marriage and have fewer children — which causes fertility rates to decline.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: As countries develop economically, fertility rates tend to decline. Women gain more access to education and employment opportunities, leading them to delay marriage and choose to have fewer children.

video_sections.label: Gender Parity, Wage Gap, and Microlending
video_sections.content_summary: Gender parity = equality between men and women (education, wages). Less development = less parity. In low-parity places: men's work (jobs, market, finances) vs women's work (domestic duties, fields, children). Even in developed countries: wage gap persists (US: women earn ~82 cents per dollar compared to men for same work). Only ~5% of world's top 500 corporations led by women. Spatial arrangement: less developed = wider wage gap; more developed = narrower but still present. Solution for developing countries: microlending — small loans to impoverished women for starting/growing small businesses. Women invest more earnings into family well-being (clothing, education) than men. Criticism: women unable to pay back loans fall into deeper poverty.

mcq.question: What is microlending?
options: Large government loans to help developing countries build infrastructure
Small loans granted to impoverished women to help them start or grow a small business
International trade agreements that reduce tariffs between wealthy and poor nations
A measurement of women's equality in education and workplace representation
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. Microlending provides small loans to impoverished women for starting or growing businesses. Women tend to invest more of their earnings into family well-being than men, so empowering them economically benefits whole societies.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: Microlending refers to small loans granted to impoverished women to help them start or grow a small business. Research shows women invest far more of their earnings than men into family well-being — so empowering women economically can change whole societies.

video_sections.label: Geography and Development
video_sections.content_summary: Geography plays a huge role in how economically developed a country is. Global North includes wealthier, more developed countries; Global South includes less developed countries — the uneven relationship between them drives the global economy. Note: ad read ~1:39-1:51.

mcq.question: What do the terms 'Global North' and 'Global South' refer to in human geography?
options: The Northern and Southern Hemispheres divided by the equator
Countries that export commodities versus countries that import them
The relative distribution of wealth and power across the world
Climate zones that determine agricultural productivity
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. Global North and Global South categorize the relative distribution of wealth and power throughout the world — the Global North includes wealthier, more developed countries while the Global South includes less developed countries.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: Global North and Global South are terms used to categorize the relative distribution of wealth and power throughout the world. The Global North includes wealthier, more developed countries, while the Global South includes the less developed countries.

video_sections.label: Rostow's Stages of Growth
video_sections.content_summary: Rostow's stages of economic growth: every country passes through 5 stages from agriculture (stage 1) to mass consumption and tertiary sector (stage 5), roughly corresponding to the Demographic Transition Model. Criticisms: too simple, based on Western/Anglo-American development patterns, assumes economies develop in isolation, and assumes no obstacles from other countries.

mcq.question: What is a major criticism of Rostow's stages of economic growth model?
options: It overemphasizes the role of imperialism in slowing development
It assumes economies develop in isolation, ignoring global interconnections
It only describes development in countries of the Global South
It argues that not all countries can reach the mass consumption stage
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. A key criticism of Rostow's model is that it assumes each country's economy develops independently, ignoring the interconnected reality of the global economy.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: A major criticism of Rostow's model is that it assumes every country's economy develops in isolation. In reality, countries' economies are deeply interconnected, and other countries can create obstacles to development — factors Rostow's model fails to account for.

video_sections.label: Dependency and World Systems
video_sections.content_summary: Dependency Theory: peripheral countries are poor not by their own fault but because of exploitative practices by core countries (neocolonialism). International division of labor requires periphery to remain at the bottom for core countries to succeed — better than Rostow because it recognizes interconnectedness. Wallerstein's World Systems Theory: world economy is an interconnected capitalist system organized into core, periphery, and semi-periphery. Core countries are most developed because they were the earliest adopters of industrialization.

mcq.question: What does Dependency Theory argue about why peripheral countries remain poor?
options: Because they haven't progressed through Rostow's five stages of development yet
Because exploitative economic practices by core countries keep them dependent and underdeveloped
Because they lack the natural resources needed for industrialization
Because they voluntarily chose to remain in the primary economic sector
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. Dependency Theory argues that peripheral countries are poor because of ongoing exploitative practices by core countries — and that core countries actually need to exploit the periphery to maintain their own economic success.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: Dependency Theory argues that peripheral countries are poor because core countries exploit them economically (neocolonialism). Unlike Rostow's theory, it recognizes that the world economy is interconnected and that core countries need periphery to remain at the bottom.

video_sections.label: Periphery and Commodity Dependence
video_sections.content_summary: Peripheral countries are least developed because imperialism and colonialism prevented them from adopting industrialization. Semi-peripheral countries (BRICS: Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa) serve as intermediaries. International division of labor: core = tertiary+, semi-periphery = primary/secondary/tertiary, periphery = primary. Commodity Dependence Theory: strong link between underdevelopment and commodity-export-dominated economies — short-term commodity prices fluctuate regularly, and long-term commodity prices steadily decrease, causing micro-economies to shrink over time.

mcq.question: According to Wallerstein's World Systems Theory, why are peripheral countries the least developed?
options: Because they voluntarily chose to focus on primary sector activities
Because they have unfavorable climates that prevent industrialization
Because imperialism and colonialism prevented them from adopting industrialization early
Because the United Nations restricts their access to global trade networks
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. Wallerstein argues that peripheral countries are least developed because their colonial past prevented them from adopting industrialization — core countries aren't special, they just started first.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: Wallerstein's World Systems Theory argues peripheral countries are least developed because imperialism and colonialism prevented them from adopting industrialization. Core countries aren't inherently special — they just started industrializing earlier and used imperial power to keep others behind.

video_sections.label: Why Countries Trade
video_sections.content_summary: Two reasons countries trade. (1) Complementarity: no one country can produce everything it needs — some are rich in natural resources, others in knowledge workers, so they complement each other. (2) Comparative advantage: a country specializes in producing goods it can make more efficiently than anyone else, selling excess on the world market — Japan invested in high-tech manufacturing (microchips, computers), China specialized in silk production. Both concepts have always created the basis for trade, and globalization has made them even more fundamental. Brief plug for note guides at ~0:39.

mcq.question: What is comparative advantage?
options: When a country can produce every good it needs without trading
When a country specializes in producing goods it can make more efficiently than anyone else
When two countries agree to charge the same tariffs on imported goods
When a country's GDP is higher than its GNP per capita
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. Comparative advantage means a country specializes in producing goods it's best at, sells the excess globally, and imports what it needs from countries with different comparative advantages.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: Comparative advantage describes when a country specializes in producing certain goods because it's better equipped and more efficient than others. By specializing, the country achieves economies of scale and trades its surplus for goods other countries produce better.

video_sections.label: Neoliberalism and Trade Blocs
video_sections.content_summary: Neoliberalism promotes free trade and reducing government intervention (like tariffs), arguing it leads to economic growth. Two trade organizations: (1) the European Union — started as a post-WWII economic agreement among six countries integrating coal and steel, grew over time, and officially formed in 1993 with 27 member countries merged into a singular economic unit. (2) Mercosur (Southern Common Market) — a South American free trade partnership where member nations removed tariffs between each other, creating a more competitive regional economic bloc.

mcq.question: What do neoliberal policies promote?
options: Increasing tariffs to protect domestic industries from foreign competition
Free trade and reducing government intervention in trade relationships
Establishing international lending organizations to restructure loan payments
Specializing in goods a country can produce more efficiently than others
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. Neoliberalism promotes free trade and reducing government intervention in trade, arguing that by removing barriers like tariffs, participating countries will experience economic growth and development.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: Neoliberalism describes the movement to promote free trade and reduce government intervention in trade relationships. Proponents argue that by reducing barriers to international trade like tariffs, participating countries will experience economic growth and development.

video_sections.label: OPEC, WTO, and Tariffs
video_sections.content_summary: Two more trade organizations: (1) OPEC — an association of the largest oil-producing countries (Saudi Arabia, Iraq) controlling over 80% of the world's oil, with enormous influence on global oil prices. (2) The WTO (World Trade Organization) — regulates trade on a global scale by negotiating deals, moderating disputes, and assisting developing countries. Tariffs — taxes on imported goods — disrupt free trade; governments impose them to protect domestic industry (making imports more expensive so consumers buy local) and as political tools (the 2018 US-China trade war where both countries imposed tariffs and both suffered economically).

mcq.question: Why do governments impose tariffs on imported goods?
options: To increase the quality of imported products available to consumers
To protect domestic industry by making imported goods more expensive, encouraging consumers to buy local products
To ensure that peripheral countries can sell more commodities on the global market
To comply with World Trade Organization requirements for international trade
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. Tariffs make imported goods more expensive, encouraging consumers to buy domestically produced goods instead — protecting domestic industry and jobs. They can also be used as political tools (trade wars).
mcq.feedback_incorrect: Tariffs are taxes on imported goods that make them more expensive. The goal is usually to protect domestic industry by persuading consumers to buy locally made products. They can also be imposed for political rivalry — like the 2018 US-China trade war.

video_sections.label: International Financial Crises
video_sections.content_summary: Downside of global interconnectedness: economic crises spread worldwide. The Great Depression (1930s) started in the US but rippled globally because of the already-globalized economy. The 2007-2008 financial crisis: US banks gave out inflated house loans, the housing bubble burst, mass loan defaults caused banks to fail, and the crisis spread worldwide through economic connections. The International Monetary Fund (IMF), created after WWII, helps protect against crises by restructuring loan payments and overhauling economies. Microlending also promotes development on a smaller scale in the developing world.

mcq.question: Why did the 2007-2008 financial crisis spread from the United States to the rest of the world?
options: Because the United States imposed tariffs on every other country simultaneously
Because economies are so tightly interconnected through globalization that one country's crisis pulls others down
Because the World Trade Organization required all countries to adopt the same banking policies
Because peripheral countries had invested all their commodity exports in American real estate
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. Because globalization has made the world's economies so tightly interconnected, when a major economy like the US experiences a crisis, the shock waves ripple across the globe — affecting banks and businesses worldwide.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: The 2007-08 crisis spread globally because economies are tightly interconnected through globalization. When US banks failed from defaulted housing loans, it affected international banks and investors connected to those loans, causing worldwide economic hardship.

video_sections.label: De-industrialization and Fordism
video_sections.content_summary: De-industrialization refers to the disappearance of industrial sectors from core countries. Fordism (post-WWII) structured economies around mass consumption and mass production of standardized goods, but by the 1970s rising domestic manufacturing costs led factory owners to build in peripheral/semi-peripheral countries with lower wages.

mcq.question: What does Fordism refer to?
options: The use of special economic zones to attract foreign manufacturing investment
An economy structured around mass consumption and mass production of standardized goods
The process of dispersing manufacturing across multiple countries
The concentration of high-tech businesses near major universities
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. Fordism, named after Henry Ford, describes the post-WWII economic structure based on mass consumption and mass production of standardized manufactured goods, primarily in core countries.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: Fordism refers to an economy structured around mass consumption and mass production of standardized manufactured goods. Named after Henry Ford, it characterized post-WWII core country economies before manufacturing costs rose in the 1970s.

video_sections.label: Rust Belt and SEZs
video_sections.content_summary: The manufacturing shift ended Fordism in core countries, creating the Rust Belt in the US and establishing today's international division of labor. Note: ad read ~2:48-3:00. Three new manufacturing zones emerged, starting with Special Economic Zones (SEZs)—geographic sectors with different business laws to attract foreign investment, used extensively by China since the 1970s.

mcq.question: What is a Special Economic Zone (SEZ)?
options: A region where all economic activity is controlled by the national government
A geographic sector within a country with different business and trade laws designed to attract foreign investment
A zone where only primary sector economic activities like mining and farming are permitted
An area designated exclusively for ecotourism and environmental protection
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. An SEZ has different business and trade laws (lower taxes, reduced customs duties) to attract foreign investment. China's use of SEZs since the 1970s helped it become the world's second largest economy.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: A Special Economic Zone is a geographic sector with different business laws designed to attract foreign investment through lower taxes and reduced regulations. China's extensive use of SEZs has been a major factor in its economic rise.

video_sections.label: EPZs, FTZs, and Post-Fordism
video_sections.content_summary: Export Processing Zones (EPZs) offer lower taxes and less strict labor laws to attract corporations for cheap production (fast fashion controversy), while Free Trade Zones (FTZs) provide tax-free storage and distribution for exports (5,000+ worldwide). The international division of labor also produced post-Fordism (manufacturing dispersed across multiple countries) and multiplier effects (one job simultaneously creates several others).

mcq.question: What is the multiplier effect in economics?
options: When commodity prices multiply due to increased tariffs on imports
When creating one job simultaneously creates several associated jobs in the community
When a country's GDP doubles because of increased manufacturing output
When informal economic sectors multiply faster than formal sectors in developing countries
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. The multiplier effect occurs when one job creates several others — a new factory doesn't just create factory workers, it also creates demand for electricians, janitors, restaurants, grocery stores, and more.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: The multiplier effect describes how creating one job simultaneously creates several associated jobs. When a car factory opens, it also creates jobs for electricians, janitors, grocery stores, restaurants, and other services to support the new workers.

video_sections.label: JIT and Agglomeration
video_sections.content_summary: Just-in-time delivery manufactures smaller batches in response to consumer demand rather than mass-producing, reducing the need for large permanent workforces and making economies of scale less important. Agglomeration economies concentrate similar businesses in the same area (e.g., Silicon Valley near airports and universities), creating growth poles/technopoles that governments seek to attract.

mcq.question: What is just-in-time delivery?
options: Mass-producing large quantities of goods and storing them in warehouses for immediate purchase
Shipping goods through free trade zones to avoid export taxes
Manufacturing smaller batches of goods in response to actual consumer demand rather than mass-producing in advance
Using agglomeration economies to concentrate production in a single geographic region
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. Just-in-time delivery involves manufacturing smaller batches of goods in response to consumer demand rather than mass-producing and storing them, reducing the need for large warehouses and permanent workforces.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: Just-in-time delivery refers to manufacturing smaller batches of goods in response to actual consumer demand rather than mass-producing and storing them in warehouses. This reduces companies' need for large permanent workforces and lowers warehouse costs.

video_sections.label: Problems: Resource Depletion and Pollution
video_sections.content_summary: Sustainability = using Earth's resources to meet today's needs while preserving them for future generations. Global economy restructured for mass consumption has increased living standards but at environmental cost. Two main problems: (1) Depletion of natural resources: manufacturing relies on non-renewable resources. Industrial agriculture threatens freshwater aquifers (used faster than replenished). Coal, iron, copper could become far more scarce within 20 years. Mass consumption habits questioned (10 pairs of shoes, plastic bags, new phone every year). (2) Pollution: factories pollute water and air. Global scale: climate change from rising CO2 since Industrial Revolution → rising ocean levels, displacing coastal populations, stronger hurricanes, more wildfires. Note: ad read ~1:50-2:00.

mcq.question: What sustainability concern does industrial agriculture create regarding freshwater?
options: It requires so much irrigation that freshwater aquifers are being depleted faster than they can replenish
It introduces saltwater into freshwater sources through soil salinization
It reduces the amount of rainfall in agricultural regions through deforestation
It causes rivers to change course, disrupting water supply to nearby cities
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. Industrial agriculture requires intensive irrigation that threatens to deplete freshwater aquifers — the water is being used up faster than it can naturally replenish itself.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: Industrial agriculture requires intensive irrigation that threatens freshwater aquifers. The water is used up faster than it can replenish — a non-renewable situation that raises serious sustainability concerns.

video_sections.label: Ecotourism
video_sections.content_summary: Ecotourism: protecting natural landscapes from industrial encroachment by creating sustainable tourist destinations. Provides economic benefits (guides, hosts, revenue for environmental protection). Galapagos Islands example: 2,000+ unique species, huge tourist income, but constant balance between preservation and tourism demand. Criticism: much money goes back to large tourism businesses in core countries, and infrastructure only impacts a small portion of the poorer country's population.

mcq.question: What is ecotourism?
options: The process of converting natural landscapes into industrial manufacturing zones
An effort to protect natural landscapes by creating sustainable tourist destinations that provide economic benefits
A government policy that bans all tourism in environmentally sensitive areas
A United Nations program that pays developing countries to stop using non-renewable resources
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. Ecotourism protects natural landscapes from industrial encroachment by creating sustainable tourist destinations — providing economic benefits to locals through jobs as guides and hosts while preserving the environment.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: Ecotourism protects natural landscapes by creating sustainable tourist destinations that provide economic benefits — locals get jobs as guides and hosts, and revenue is allocated for environmental protection. The Galapagos Islands is a major example.

video_sections.label: UN Sustainable Development Goals
video_sections.content_summary: The UN responded to environmental and social concerns in 2000 by releasing Sustainable Development Goals — covering environmental sustainability, poverty alleviation, gender equity, and more. These goals re-envision development from purely economic to a holistic approach considering all dimensions of human flourishing. Progress has been slow (Denmark most progress, sub-Saharan Africa least), but renewable power and ecotourism are becoming more prevalent.

mcq.question: How do the UN Sustainable Development Goals re-envision the concept of development?
options: They focus exclusively on reducing carbon emissions through industrial regulations
They shift development from a purely economic measure to a holistic approach considering environmental sustainability, poverty, and gender equity
They replace national development plans with a single standardized UN economic model
They require developed countries to stop all manufacturing until developing countries catch up
mcq.feedback_correct: Correct. The UN Sustainable Development Goals re-envision development from being purely economic to a holistic approach that considers environmental sustainability, poverty alleviation, gender equity, and other dimensions of human flourishing.
mcq.feedback_incorrect: The UN Sustainable Development Goals re-envision development from being purely economic to a holistic approach — including environmental sustainability, poverty alleviation, and gender equity. Progress has been slow, with Denmark leading and sub-Saharan Africa lagging behind.

these are considered universalizing religions because each of those belief systems can be planted and grow in any culture in the world so Christianity and Kenya will retain the essentials of Christian
